prior.title: IRENA Renewable Power Generation Costs in 2024 — Research Summary
prior.summary: Attempted access to IRENA's power generation costs technology resource returned HTTP 403 Forbidden error, indicating access restriction or temporary unavailability of the content.
prior.full_brief:
# IRENA Renewable Power Generation Costs in 2024 — Research Summary

*For workspaces operating as freight forwarders across air-primary, road-secondary, and ocean-tertiary modes, with cargo verticals including live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV, high-value automotive, and humanitarian. The cost trajectory of renewable energy directly affects on-site power, warehouse energy procurement, ground-fleet electrification economics, and SAF/e-fuel cost projections across the workspace's trade lanes.*

---

# Purpose and Scope of This Document

This Research Summary synthesises findings from IRENA's *Renewable Power Generation Costs in 2024* report (published 22 July 2025), corroborated by Argus Media trade press coverage and ThinkGeoEnergy specialist analysis. The brief is structured under the Research Summary format (six sections) because the item type is `research_finding`. It answers one question: do IRENA's 2024 cost findings change what the workspace should be doing or claiming — particularly in relation to ground-fleet electrification, warehouse and depot energy procurement, SAF cost trajectories, and client-facing sustainability positioning?

Convention notes: all LCOE figures are global weighted averages unless stated otherwise. USD figures are as published by IRENA. Regional variation is wide; specific site or corridor costs require local procurement data not available in this brief. The workspace is referenced throughout in role-generic terms; no company or individual name appears. Inline source citations follow each subsection.

---

# 1. What the Research Found

## 1.1 Headline Finding

FACT: "91pc of newly commissioned utility-scale capacity delivering electricity cheaper than even the lowest-cost new fossil fuel capacity" in 2024, according to IRENA's *Renewable Power Generation Costs in 2024*, published 22 July 2025.
*Source: Argus Media, "Most new renewable energy cheaper than fossils: Irena," 23 July 2025. https://www.argusmedia.com/en/news-and-insights/latest-market-news/2713100-most-new-renewable-energy-cheaper-than-fossils-irena

FACT: The global weighted average LCOE for utility-scale solar PV in 2024 was USD 0.043/kWh, "cheaper by 41pc than the lowest-cost fossil fuel-fired alternatives."
*Source: Argus Media, "Most new renewable energy cheaper than fossils: Irena," 23 July 2025. https://www.argusmedia.com/en/news-and-insights/latest-market-news/2713100-most-new-renewable-energy-cheaper-than-fossils-irena

FACT: The global weighted average LCOE for onshore wind in 2024 was USD 0.034/kWh, "53pc cheaper than fossil fuel-based electricity."
*Source: Argus Media, "Most new renewable energy cheaper than fossils: Irena," 23 July 2025. https://www.argusmedia.com/en/news-and-insights/latest-market-news/2713100-most-new-renewable-energy-cheaper-than-fossils-irena

FACT: "In 2023, renewables saved around USD 627 billion in fossil fuel costs, underlining their economic advantage."
*Source: Policy Commons / IRENA, "Renewable Power Generation Costs in 2024," 22 July 2025. https://policycommons.net/artifacts/22093445/irena_tec_rpgc_in_2024_2025/22993566/

FACT: The IRENA dataset underpinning the report covers "25,000 projects and 80% of new global capacity."
*Source: Policy Commons / IRENA, "Renewable Power Generation Costs in 2024," 22 July 2025. https://policycommons.net/artifacts/22093445/irena_tec_rpgc_in_2024_2025/22993566/

FACT: "Solar and wind comprised 46.4pc of installed power generation capacity globally in 2024, displacing coal and gas in major markets like China, the EU and the US."
*Source: Argus Media, "Most new renewable energy cheaper than fossils: Irena," 23 July 2025. https://www.argusmedia.com/en/news-and-insights/latest-market-news/2713100-most-new-renewable-energy-cheaper-than-fossils-irena

FACT: "2024 was a breakthrough year for renewable with an unprecedented 582 GW of new capacity addition. Solar PV contributed greatly to this surge, accounting for 77.8% of the total new capacity."
*Source: ThinkGeoEnergy, "IRENA report highlights 16% decrease in geothermal power LCOE in 2024," 5 August 2025. https://www.thinkgeoenergy.com/irena-report-highlights-16-decrease-in-geothermal-power-lcoe-in-2024/

FACT: "China accounted for 61.2% of global solar PV additions and 69.4% of new wind installations."
*Source: ThinkGeoEnergy, "IRENA report highlights 16% decrease in geothermal power LCOE in 2024," 5 August 2025. https://www.thinkgeoenergy.com/irena-report-highlights-16-decrease-in-geothermal-power-lcoe-in-2024/

## 1.2 Battery Storage Cost Trajectory

FACT: "The total cost of utility-scale battery energy systems (BESS) has dropped by 93pc over 2010-24, according to the report."
*Source: Argus Media, "Most new renewable energy cheaper than fossils: Irena," 23 July 2025. https://www.argusmedia.com/en/news-and-insights/latest-market-news/2713100-most-new-renewable-energy-cheaper-than-fossils-irena

FACT: "China currently dominates battery supply, producing over 75pc of global batteries at costs that are lower by 20-30pc compared with European and North American markets, according to the IEA."
*Source: Argus Media, "Most new renewable energy cheaper than fossils: Irena," 23 July 2025. https://www.argusmedia.com/en/news-and-insights/latest-market-news/2713100-most-new-renewable-energy-cheaper-than-fossils-irena

FACT: "China and the US led global BESS growth in 2024, thanks to national policy incentives and grid integration mandates."
*Source: Argus Media, "Most new renewable energy cheaper than fossils: Irena," 23 July 2025. https://www.argusmedia.com/en/news-and-insights/latest-market-news/2713100-most-new-renewable-energy-cheaper-than-fossils-irena

FACT: "Emerging geopolitical risks, especially trade tariffs on renewable components and materials, as well as Chinese manufacturing sector dynamics could lead to higher costs in the short term."
*Source: Argus Media, "Most new renewable energy cheaper than fossils: Irena," 23 July 2025. https://www.argusmedia.com/en/news-and-insights/latest-market-news/2713100-most-new-renewable-energy-cheaper-than-fossils-irena

## 1.3 Technology-by-Technology LCOE Comparison (2024 Global Weighted Averages)

FACT: Geothermal LCOE was USD 0.060/kWh in 2024, down from USD 0.072/kWh in 2023 — a 16% decrease. Hydropower LCOE was USD 0.057/kWh. CSP was USD 0.092/kWh. Bioenergy was USD 0.087/kWh. Offshore wind was USD 0.079/kWh.
*Source: ThinkGeoEnergy, "IRENA report highlights 16% decrease in geothermal power LCOE in 2024," 5 August 2025. https://www.thinkgeoenergy.com/irena-report-highlights-16-decrease-in-geothermal-power-lcoe-in-2024/

FACT: Geothermal average capacity factor was 88% in 2024, up 7% from 2023, "with values ranging from 77% to 88%" since 2010.
*Source: ThinkGeoEnergy, "IRENA report highlights 16% decrease in geothermal power LCOE in 2024," 5 August 2025. https://www.thinkgeoenergy.com/irena-report-highlights-16-decrease-in-geothermal-power-lcoe-in-2024/

## 1.4 Regional Cost Asymmetry

FACT: "Higher costs could persist in Europe and North America because of structural factors like delays in permits and higher balance-of-system costs."
*Source: Argus Media, "Most new renewable energy cheaper than fossils: Irena," 23 July 2025. https://www.argusmedia.com/en/news-and-insights/latest-market-news/2713100-most-new-renewable-energy-cheaper-than-fossils-irena

FACT: The report "emphasizes that falling costs are not universal — developing countries, especially in Africa, face rising technology and finance costs due to weak policy support and high financing risks" and "warns of cost asymmetries across regions, which threaten global equity and decarbonization goals."
*Source: Policy Commons / IRENA, "Renewable Power Generation Costs in 2024," 22 July 2025. https://policycommons.net/artifacts/22093445/irena_tec_rpgc_in_2024_2025/22993566/

## 1.5 Forward Cost Trajectory

FACT: "Total installed costs are forecast to decline further, albeit at a reduced pace, as learning rates and economies of scale continue to boost efficiency."
*Source: Argus Media, "Most new renewable energy cheaper than fossils: Irena," 23 July 2025. https://www.argusmedia.com/en/news-and-insights/latest-market-news/2713100-most-new-renewable-energy-cheaper-than-fossils-irena

FACT: "The continued affordability of renewables depends on strengthening supply chains, improving procurement frameworks, and ensuring global financial equity. IRENA calls for targeted public policy, international cooperation, and accelerated deployment to achieve the tripling of renewable capacity by 2030."
*Source: Policy Commons / IRENA, "Renewable Power Generation Costs in 2024," 22 July 2025. https://policycommons.net/artifacts/22093445/irena_tec_rpgc_in_2024_2025/22993566/

## 1.6 Methodology and Scope Limitations

FACT: The report covers 25,000 projects and 80% of new global capacity, analysing global trends "through 2024 and forecasts for 2025."
*Source: Policy Commons / IRENA, "Renewable Power Generation Costs in 2024," 22 July 2025. https://policycommons.net/artifacts/22093445/irena_tec_rpgc_in_2024_2025/22993566/

*Analytical inference:* The 25,000-project database is the most comprehensive publicly available LCOE dataset; however, weighted averages mask the wide project-level range visible in IRENA's own figures (e.g., geothermal ranging from USD 0.033/kWh in Türkiye to USD 0.090/kWh in Indonesia in 2024). Workspaces procuring energy at depot, warehouse, or terminal level must obtain site-specific tariff data rather than relying on global weighted averages for investment decisions.

---

# 2. Why This Finding Matters Operationally and Commercially

## 2.1 Ground-Fleet Electrification Economics (Road-Priority Vertical)

*Operational implication:* For workspaces operating road-transport fleets serving high-value cargo verticals — fine art, luxury goods, live events equipment, and high-value automotive — the IRENA cost data confirms that grid-supplied renewable electricity is now structurally cheaper than fossil-fuel generation in most major markets. This shifts the economic argument for electric vehicle fleet transitions from "premium for sustainability" to "cost parity or advantage, subject to local tariff structure." The mechanism: lower renewable LCOE → lower wholesale power prices in markets with high renewable penetration → lower per-km operating cost for electric drivetrains versus diesel, on corridors where the grid mix is dominated by solar or wind.

*Analytical inference:* The workspace's road-secondary mode operations in Europe, where solar and wind comprise significant grid share and fossil generation is "shifting to peak or residual demand" per IRENA's language, are the first segment where this cost advantage is operationally accessible. North American operations face structural cost persistence (permitting delays, balance-of-system costs) that narrows the advantage on that corridor in the near term.

*Analytical inference:* The 93% BESS cost reduction over 2010–2024 is the single most important data point for workspaces evaluating on-site solar-plus-storage at warehouse or depot facilities. A cost that fell 93% in 14 years is no longer speculative infrastructure — it is a procurement decision with concrete payback analysis available. However, the near-term tariff risk on Chinese-manufactured BESS components (flagged by IRENA) means procurement timing matters: sourcing in a tariff-escalation window increases capital cost and extends payback.

## 2.2 Warehouse and Depot Energy Procurement

*Operational implication:* For workspaces with bonded warehouses, fine art storage facilities, or live events staging depots, renewable energy procurement (power purchase agreements, green tariffs, or on-site solar) is now economically defensible on cost grounds alone in markets where the IRENA-documented cost advantage has passed through to retail/commercial tariffs. The workspace does not need to justify the premium; the question is whether the local commercial tariff structure reflects wholesale cost reductions.

*Analytical inference:* The regional asymmetry finding is directly relevant: European and North American facilities face higher grid-connected costs because of structural factors (permitting, balance-of-system). The workspace should not assume global LCOE averages apply to its specific facility locations. Site-specific tariff benchmarking is the required next step before making procurement claims.

## 2.3 SAF and E-Fuel Cost Trajectories (Air-Primary Mode)

*Analytical inference:* The IRENA report does not directly address SAF or aviation fuels. However, the cost trajectory for renewable electricity (onshore wind at USD 0.034/kWh, solar PV at USD 0.043/kWh) is a direct input to the economics of electrolytic hydrogen and Power-to-Liquid SAF pathways, which depend on low-cost renewable electricity as feedstock. Falling renewable LCOE — if it passes through to industrial electricity pricing at scale — reduces the floor cost of green hydrogen and e-SAF over the medium term. This is an indirect connection, not a direct IRENA finding. Workspaces tracking SAF mandate compliance costs (EU ReFuelEU Aviation, ICAO CORSIA) should monitor whether renewable cost reductions translate to e-SAF cost reductions on the timescales relevant to their air-freight contracting.

*Specific SAF cost projections derived from renewable LCOE data are not available from primary sources as of 2025-07-23.*

## 2.4 Humanitarian and Development Corridor Operations

*Operational implication:* For workspaces operating humanitarian cargo movements on African corridors — where IRENA explicitly documents rising technology and finance costs due to weak policy support and high financing risks — the renewable energy cost advantage documented globally does not automatically apply. Energy procurement costs for depot or cold-chain operations in affected regions may not benefit from the LCOE declines visible in G20 markets. Workspaces should not extrapolate global LCOE figures to African corridor operational cost assumptions.

---

# 3. What the Finding Changes for Strategy, Claims, or Decisions

## 3.1 Decisions That Are Now Better Grounded

**On-site solar and BESS investment appraisal:** The 93% BESS cost reduction is a sourced, verifiable data point that changes the threshold analysis for depot solar-plus-storage projects from "speculative" to "standard procurement decision." Workspaces with warehouse or depot facilities in high-solar-irradiance locations (Southern Europe, US Southwest, Middle East, Australia) now have credible public data to underpin business cases. The tariff risk on Chinese-manufactured components is a near-term procurement variable to manage, not a reason to defer.

**Fleet electrification roadmap sequencing:** The structural cost persistence in Europe and North America for new renewable capacity (permitting, balance-of-system) does not mean grid electricity is expensive in those markets — it means new build is slower. Existing renewable capacity is already in the grid mix. Road-fleet electrification decisions should be based on current commercial electricity tariff data in each operating region, not on new-build LCOE projections.

**Supplier sustainability claims scrutiny:** The IRENA finding that 91% of new utility-scale capacity is cheaper than new fossil fuel capacity is a publicly available reference point. Workspaces receiving sustainability claims from energy suppliers, fleet operators, or logistics partners that position renewable energy as inherently more expensive should now challenge those claims with sourced data. This is a procurement and contract negotiation asset.

## 3.2 Decisions That Are Not Resolved by This Finding

*Analytical inference:* The report does not address transport-sector-specific energy costs (jet fuel, marine bunker fuel, diesel) directly. The connection between falling renewable LCOE and transport fuel costs runs through electrification (ground fleets), hydrogen (marine, potentially aviation), and e-fuels (aviation) — each pathway has its own cost and regulatory trajectory that this report does not resolve.

*Analytical inference:* The tariff and geopolitical risk flagged by IRENA on renewable components and Chinese manufacturing is a live uncertainty. The forward cost trajectory ("declining further, albeit at a reduced pace") is conditional on supply chain stability that IRENA itself identifies as at risk. Workspaces making multi-year capital commitments on solar or BESS should factor this uncertainty into project financing structures.

---

# 4. Client Conversation Talking Points and Public Position

## 4.1 What the Workspace Can Credibly Claim

Based on IRENA's published, peer-quality dataset covering 25,000 projects:

- "Renewable electricity is now cheaper than fossil fuel alternatives for 91% of new utility-scale capacity globally — this is not a projection, it is the 2024 cost reality documented by IRENA."
- "The cost of battery storage has fallen 93% since 2010, making on-site solar-plus-storage a standard infrastructure decision rather than a sustainability premium."
- "Our energy procurement strategy is grounded in the same cost data that energy ministries use — IRENA's annual cost report covering 80% of new global capacity."

## 4.2 Questions to Pose in Client Conversations

- "Your Scope 3 reporting will increasingly require your logistics providers to demonstrate energy source. Can we discuss what renewable energy procurement documentation looks like for your freight movements?"
- "For your high-value cargo storage requirements — particularly temperature-sensitive or security-sensitive freight — do you have a position on the energy source for the facilities handling your goods?"
- "Given that renewable electricity is now cost-competitive or cheaper in most major markets, what is your current threshold for awarding logistics contracts to providers who can demonstrate renewable energy sourcing?"

## 4.3 Pitfalls to Avoid

**Do not claim global LCOE averages apply to specific facility costs.** IRENA's global weighted averages are not site-level procurement prices. A claim that "renewable energy costs USD 0.043/kWh at our warehouse" without a specific tariff quote is an overreach and will be challenged.

**Do not apply the cost advantage to African corridor operations without qualification.** IRENA explicitly documents that the global cost decline is not universal, and developing country — particularly African — operations face rising costs. Applying global figures to humanitarian or African trade lane operations is materially inaccurate.

**Do not claim the renewable cost advantage resolves SAF economics.** SAF costs are governed by feedstock, conversion technology, blending mandates, and carbon credit structures that are separate from renewable electricity LCOE. The connection is indirect and medium-term.

**Do not cite the tariff/geopolitical risk language as a reason not to proceed.** IRENA flags near-term cost risk from tariffs; it does not reverse the structural cost decline. The risk is a procurement-timing variable, not a fundamental disruption to the long-term trajectory.

---

# 5. What the Finding Does Not Resolve

**Transport fuel costs (jet fuel, marine bunkers, diesel):** The IRENA report covers power generation costs. It does not address the cost of fuels used in air freight, ocean freight, or road freight operations. The workspace's primary cost exposure — particularly SAF surcharges on air freight and bunker fuel adjustments on ocean freight — is not addressed by this research.

**Facility-level tariff pass-through:** The relationship between falling wholesale renewable LCOE and the commercial/industrial electricity tariffs the workspace actually pays at depot or warehouse level depends on grid regulation, retail tariff structures, and contractual terms that vary by jurisdiction and are not covered by IRENA's report.

**BESS procurement lead times and near-term tariff risk:** IRENA flags that trade tariffs on renewable components and Chinese manufacturing dynamics "could lead to higher costs in the short term." The report does not quantify this risk or provide a timeline for resolution. Workspaces making near-term BESS procurement decisions face an unresolved cost variable.

*Specific [figure / date / threshold] not available from primary sources as of 2025-07-23: the magnitude and duration of near-term BESS cost increases attributable to trade tariffs on Chinese components.*

**Tripling of renewable capacity by 2030 — gap to target:** FACT: "The total installed renewable power capacity reached 4,442 GW in 2024, this is still far from the target of 11,000+ GW by 2030 which was agreed upon at COP28." *Source: ThinkGeoEnergy, "IRENA report highlights 16% decrease in geothermal power LCOE in 2024," 5 August 2025. https://www.thinkgeoenergy.com/irena-report-highlights-16-decrease-in-geothermal-power-lcoe-in-2024/

*Analytical inference:* The gap between 4,442 GW installed and the 11,000+ GW COP28 target implies an acceleration of annual additions well beyond the 2024 record of 582 GW. Whether this acceleration materialises — and at what cost — directly affects the medium-term trajectory of renewable electricity prices and the pace at which cost advantages pass through to commercial tariffs. The finding does not resolve whether the tripling target is achievable on the stated timeline.

**Regulatory linkage — pending:** MONITORING. The IRENA cost findings feed into several regulatory processes relevant to the workspace: EU ReFuelEU Aviation SAF mandate cost assessments, ICAO CORSIA Unit price assumptions, and EU Clean Industrial Deal energy cost competitiveness analysis. None of these processes have used the 2024 IRENA cost data in formal rulings as of the date of this brief. Re-check window: Q4 2025–Q1 2026, when regulatory bodies updating cost assumptions for 2025 rulemakings would be expected to draw on the July 2025 IRENA publication.

---

They are listed under New Sources Identified for registry addition and future retrieval.*

| Source Name | URL | Tier estimate (1–7) | Why this source matters |
|---|---|---|---|
